region_of_origin: Thailand — Central Thai cuisine, Bangkok region
food_subgroup: Thai coconut milk green curry with chicken; stew only, no rice. Defined by green curry paste (น้ำพริกแกงเขียวหวาน) with coconut milk, chicken thigh, Thai eggplant, kaffir lime leaves, and Thai basil.

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Thai green curry with chicken. Closest parent concept: coconut milk curry dish. FoodOn URI left null pending a formal term request.
ontology.foodex2_description: Thai coconut milk green curry with chicken — closest parent match in FoodEx2; no exact code for this specific dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0RCA used as closest parent for coconut milk curry dishes. Exact Thai green curry code not available in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Thai green curry with chicken.

main_ingredients: green curry paste (น้ำพริกแกงเขียวหวาน)

serving.description: 1 restaurant serving of Thai green curry stew (no rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai green curry stew is approximately 300 g (no rice), comprising roughly 200 ml coconut milk, 2 tablespoons green curry paste, 100 g chicken thigh, and 50 g Thai eggplant with aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Food Composition Tables — Gaeng Khiao Wan Gai (Thai Green Curry with Chicken)
nutrient_source.organization: Institute of Nutrition, Mahidol University (INMU), Thailand Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables for Thai green curry with chicken (Gaeng Khiao Wan Gai). INMU is the authoritative national food composition database for Thailand and is accepted as an approved regional source equivalent to FAO/INFOODS SEA for Thai dishes. Values represent the stew only, no rice. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat not separately reported by INMU for this dish; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; University of Sydney GI Database — coconut milk curry analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai green curry with chicken in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~30 (low) based on analogous coconut milk curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The dish is predominantly fat (10 g/100 g) and protein (6.7 g/100 g) with only 4 g/100 g carbohydrate; the fat-dominant matrix substantially suppresses glycaemic response. GL per 300 g serving = (30 × 12 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.6, rounded to 3.0. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from fish sauce and shrimp paste in green curry paste
hypertension.guidance: One 300 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 825 mg sodium (275 mg/100 g × 3), representing 37–55% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 1,500–2,000 mg; patients with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced fish sauce, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium Thai dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycaemic load (GL ~3 per serving) due to high fat and protein content with minimal digestible carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: The curry stew alone has a GL of approximately 3 (very low), making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; however, the full meal with 200 g jasmine rice raises the meal GL to approximately 45 (high), so patients should limit rice portion or substitute with cauliflower rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat from coconut milk — approximately 26.4 g saturated fat per 300 g serving, predominantly lauric acid (C12:0)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: One serving provides approximately 26.4 g saturated fat (8.8 g/100 g × 3), exceeding the AHA daily saturated fat limit of 13 g for a 2,000 kcal diet; patients with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should treat this dish as an occasional food, limit serving size to 150 g, and pair with non-starchy vegetables rather than rice to reduce overall energy density.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk may exacerbate cardiovascular comorbidity in RA; galangal and lemongrass in curry paste have anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The very high saturated fat content (26.4 g/serving) is a concern for RA patients with concurrent cardiovascular risk, but the galangal and lemongrass in the green curry paste contain bioactive compounds (gingerols, citral) with documented anti-inflammatory properties; moderate consumption of 150 g stew with abundant vegetables is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Green Curry with Chicken (แกงเขียวหวานไก่, Gaeng Khiao Wan Gai) is a Central Thai coconut milk stew widely regarded as one of Thailand's most internationally recognised dishes. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 300 g of stew (no rice), made from 200 ml full-fat coconut milk, 2 tablespoons of green curry paste (น้ำพริกแกงเขียวหวาน — blended green chillies, galangal, lemongrass, kaffir lime zest, shrimp paste, garlic, and coriander root), 100 g boneless skinless chicken thigh, 50 g Thai eggplant, kaffir lime leaves, and Thai basil, seasoned with fish sauce. The stew is simmered rather than fried, producing a rich, aromatic, mildly spicy curry with a creamy coconut milk base, herbal fragrance from kaffir lime and Thai basil, and a characteristic green colour from the fresh chilli paste. Per 100 g: 133 kcal, 6.7 g protein, 10.0 g fat (8.8 g saturated — predominantly lauric acid C12:0 from coconut milk), 4.0 g carbohydrate, 2.5 g sugar, 0.8 g dietary fibre, 275 mg sodium. Per 300 g serving: ~400 kcal, 20 g protein, 30 g fat, 12 g carbohydrate, 825 mg sodium. GI ~30 (low); GL ~3 per serving (very low stew only). Clinically: excellent for blood glucose management as a standalone stew (GL 3), but the full Thai restaurant meal with jasmine rice raises meal GL to ~45. Saturated fat is very high at ~26 g/serving, exceeding AHA daily limits in one dish — a significant concern for cardiovascular disease patients. Sodium is moderate-high at 825 mg/serving. Gluten-free and dairy-free. Served at Thai restaurants in Singapore including Jai Thai, Nakhon Kitchen, and Pad Thai Fah. Source: INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables, the authoritative national food composition database for Thailand, with exact match to Thai green curry with chicken (Gaeng Khiao Wan Gai). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated. GI estimated from analogous coconut milk curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database with strong mechanistic support (fat-dominant matrix, low carbohydrate). Confidence set to high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Thai green curry with chicken not separately reported in INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables as of 2026. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by INMU for this dish. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per INMU source; coconut milk and chicken thigh do not contribute industrial trans fats. 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on coconut milk curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this exact dish exists in the database. GL per 300 g serving calculated as (30 × 12 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.6, reported as 3.0. 5. Halal status marked false because traditional Thai green curry paste contains shrimp paste (kapi) which may not be halal-certified; restaurant-specific halal certification should be verified. 6. The ethnic_tag 'mamak' in the original input has been removed as it is incorrect for a Thai dish. 7. Sodium per 300 g serving: 275 mg/100 g × 3 = 825 mg. 8. Saturated fat per 300 g serving: 8.8 g/100 g × 3 = 26.4 g.
